phases.label: Gather Preferences
phases.description: Collect mandatory trip requirements: dates/window, origin, group, budget/currency, destination intent, plus useful constraints/defaults

phases.label: Shortlist Destination/Place Options
phases.description: Present choice-first option cards: 3-5 broad destinations or 8-10 places inside a chosen country/region, each with fit, days, logistics, budget, season, and tradeoff

phases.label: Select Places
phases.description: Let the user choose places from the option menu before researching detailed activities

phases.label: Research Activity Options
phases.description: For selected places, present 4-6 real activity/experience options per place, grouped by practicality, and ask the user to choose/approve

phases.label: Plan Itinerary
phases.description: Build a day-by-day itinerary from approved places and activities with transit, meals, check-in/out, and downtime

phases.label: Research Accommodation & Flights
phases.description: Present 4-6 flight options and 4-6 lodging options per overnight location when available, with live-data caveats and tradeoffs

phases.label: Final Plan
phases.description: Reconcile selected choices into the complete itinerary, transport plan, budget, map-ready stops, and essential information

destinationResearch.nextUserAction: Itinerary approved. Research accommodation and flights.

areasToStay.bookingTips: Book early for cherry-blossom season via Booking.com; mid-range business hotels offer great value.
areasToStay.description: Vibrant youth-culture hub with excellent JR and metro connectivity. Ideal for food, shopping, and nightlife with moderate pace.
areasToStay.nearbyTransport: JR Yamanote Line, Tokyo Metro Ginza/Hanzomon/Fukutoshin lines; direct airport bus and Narita Express nearby.
areasToStay.reviews: Highly rated by couples for walkability and dining density; noise can vary by street.
areasToStay.safetyTips: Very safe; main crossings are crowded late—stay aware of belongings in dense areas.

areasToStay.bookingTips: West/East Shinjuku hotels differ in vibe—west is calmer, east is livelier. Compare on Booking.com.
areasToStay.description: Major transit and entertainment nucleus with endless dining alleys and department stores. Strong mid-range hotel cluster.
areasToStay.nearbyTransport: JR Yamanote, Chuo, Sobu lines; Keio and Odakyu terminals; Tokyo Metro; Narita Express and airport limousine bus.
areasToStay.reviews: Praised for convenience; some blocks are busy and slightly gritty but safe.
areasToStay.safetyTips: Kabukicho area is generally safe but stick to main streets late at night; avoid touts.

areasToStay.bookingTips: Splurge options abound; mid-range business hotels are tucked side-street. Book via Booking.com.
areasToStay.description: Upscale district near theaters, galleries, and world-famous food markets. Fits food + culture themes.
areasToStay.nearbyTransport: Tokyo Metro Ginza/Marunouchi/Hibiya lines; Tsukiji Station; short taxi/subway to Tsukiji Outer Market.
areasToStay.reviews: Ideal for culinary travelers; less nightlife than Shibuya/Shinjuku but refined atmosphere.
areasToStay.safetyTips: Among Tokyo’s safest and cleanest districts; very pedestrian-friendly.

areasToStay.bookingTips: Ryokan and guesthouses mix with modern hotels; great value for culture-focused travelers on Booking.com.
areasToStay.description: Historic downtown with traditional temples, old-school eateries, and a slower neighborhood feel. Budget-friendly.
areasToStay.nearbyTransport: Tokyo Metro Ginza Line (Asakusa Station); Tobu Skytree line; water bus to Odaiba/Hamamatsucho.
areasToStay.reviews: Beloved by cultural travelers; fewer late-night options but deeply atmospheric.
areasToStay.safetyTips: Safe and family-friendly; narrow lanes are well-lit; daytime crowds near Sensoji thin out early evening.

areasToStay.bookingTips: Strong selection of mid-range chain hotels near Ueno Station on Booking.com.
areasToStay.description: Museum-dense park district plus electronics/otaku culture north. Good transit and moderate prices.
areasToStay.nearbyTransport: JR Yamanote, Keisei Skyliner (Narita access), Tokyo Metro Hibiya/Ginza lines; Shinkansen not direct but easy transfers.
areasToStay.reviews: Great for museum lovers and first-timers wanting direct Narita access; slightly less chic than Shibuya/Ginza.
areasToStay.safetyTips: Standard urban awareness; Akihabara is lively and safe, though some side-street solicitations occur.

areasToStay.bookingTips: Traditional ryokan fill fast in April—book months ahead via Booking.com; hotels near Gion-Shijo are a good compromise.
areasToStay.description: Classic geisha quarter and temple corridor. Peak culture immersion; excellent for moderate-paced walking itineraries.
areasToStay.nearbyTransport: Keihan Line (Gion-Shijo/Kiyomizu-Gojo); city buses; 20–30 min taxi/train from Kyoto Station.
areasToStay.reviews: Top-rated for atmosphere and proximity to Yasaka Shrine, Kiyomizudera, and preserved streets.
areasToStay.safetyTips: Extremely safe; respect quiet rules in residential temple zones early morning and evening.

areasToStay.bookingTips: Mid-range properties offer excellent value near Shijo-Kawaramachi; compare on Booking.com.
areasToStay.description: Central shopping and dining grid with strong subway/bus links. Great food access and modern hotels.
areasToStay.nearbyTransport: Hankyu/Kawaramachi; Subway Shijo/Karasuma; extensive city bus network.
areasToStay.reviews: Convenient base for restaurant hopping and day trips; less traditional charm than Gion but highly practical.
areasToStay.safetyTips: Very safe; standard city-center caution in busy covered arcades.

areasToStay.bookingTips: Towers like Hotel Granvia sit atop the station; surrounding blocks have newer mid-range chains on Booking.com.
areasToStay.description: Modern, ultra-connected zone with Japan’s second-largest station complex. Best for arrivals/departures and Shinkansen day trips.
areasToStay.nearbyTransport: Shinkansen to Tokyo/Osaka; JR Kyoto Line; Kintetsu; city buses; Haruka express to Kansai Airport (KIX).
areasToStay.reviews: Praised for luggage ease and transit; slightly less “Kyoto ambiance” than eastern districts.
areasToStay.safetyTips: Very safe and well-policed; expansive station can be confusing—follow signage to hotel exits.

areasToStay.bookingTips: Boutique ryokan and resorts dominate; book early for April foliage season via Booking.com.
areasToStay.description: Scenic western district along the river and bamboo grove. Resort-like, nature-leaning stay with strong cultural sites.
areasToStay.nearbyTransport: JR Sagano Line (Saga-Arashiyama); Randen tram; 25–35 min from Kyoto Station.
areasToStay.reviews: Romantic and photogenic; requires more transit time to eastern temples but rewards with tranquility.
areasToStay.safetyTips: Quiet and safe; rural edges are dark at night—stick to main lodging streets after evening.

areasToStay.bookingTips: Small inns and guesthouses available; limited big-brand hotels—check Booking.com early.
areasToStay.description: Calm temple path district with Philosophers’ Path and fewer crowds. Ideal for culture lovers seeking a slower pace.
areasToStay.nearbyTransport: City bus from Kyoto Station (~30 min); Eizan Electric Railway for Kurama/Kibune day trips.
areasToStay.reviews: Loved by repeat visitors for authenticity; fewer restaurants open late than downtown.
areasToStay.safetyTips: Very safe residential area; some paths are dimly lit after dark—plan dinner near lodging or transit.

accommodationResearch.liveDataCaveat: Rates are typical ranges for early-April 2026 based on historical pricing and seasonal demand patterns. Actual availability and prices require live confirmation on Booking.com closer to travel. Cherry blossom season increases demand and prices.

flightResearch.liveDataCaveat: Fares shown are approximate ranges for travel in early April 2026 derived from historical pricing and carrier schedules. Airlines typically release seats ~330 days in advance; live prices on Google Flights and Skyscanner may vary significantly. Baggage allowances, change fees, and fuel surcharges apply and differ by fare class.

sample_options.baggageCaveat: Typically includes 2 free checked bags (23 kg each) in base international economy; premium classes higher.
sample_options.caveat: Premium direct option; cherry-blossom season demand pushes fares toward the upper end.
sample_options.label: Direct Round-Trip (Most Comfortable)
sample_options.notes: Nonstop service between SFO and Tokyo-area airports. Minimal jet lag impact and fastest total elapsed time. Strong reliability record.

sample_options.baggageCaveat: Basic Economy includes carry-on only; standard economy adds 1 free checked bag.
sample_options.caveat: Direct competitor to JAL/ANA; often priced slightly lower but seats can be denser on older frames.
sample_options.label: Direct Round-Trip (Value Alternative)
sample_options.notes: Star Alliance nonstop to NRT. Good for MileagePlus earners and travelers seeking direct flight without Japanese-carrier premium.

sample_options.airline: Delta / Alaska (codeshare via SEA or LAX)
sample_options.baggageCaveat: Checked bag usually $60–75 each-way on basic fare; Main Cabin includes 1 bag.
sample_options.caveat: Adds 3–6 hours each way; connection risk during spring weather.
sample_options.label: One-Stop (Budget Choice)
sample_options.notes: Likely lowest published fare. Connection via West Coast hub. Good for cost-sensitive travelers willing to trade time for savings.
sample_options.route: SFO → SEA/LAX → NRT/HND — depart 2026-04-05; return via same hub 2026-04-14

sample_options.baggageCaveat: Same generous baggage policy as round-trip; confirm multi-city rules at booking.
sample_options.caveat: Best itinerary fit but open-jaw inventory can be slightly more limited than simple round-trip.
sample_options.label: Open-Jaw: SFO→Tokyo, Osaka/KIX→SFO (Best Itinerary Fit)
sample_options.notes: Fly into Tokyo and home from Kansai International (KIX) near Kyoto/Osaka. Eliminates backtracking to Tokyo and saves a travel day/shinkansen ticket.
sample_options.route: SFO → NRT/HND on 2026-04-05; KIX → SFO on 2026-04-14

sample_options.baggageCaveat: Standard economy includes 1 checked bag; basic fares may not.
sample_options.caveat: YVR connections can be smooth but US preclearance in Canada adds steps; schedule dependent.
sample_options.label: One-Stop via Vancouver (Flexible Alternative)
sample_options.notes: Potential for combining with stopover programs. Good aeroplan/redemption option. Slightly longer routing but competitive pricing.
sample_options.route: SFO → YVR → NRT (or HND) — depart 2026-04-05; return via YVR 2026-04-14

sample_options.baggageCaveat: Low-cost or hybrid model: checked bags, seat selection, and meals cost extra (~$100–250 add-ons).
sample_options.caveat: Schedule may include late-night departures or less daily frequency; customer-service tradeoffs vs legacy carriers.
sample_options.label: Low-Cost / Hybrid (Lowest Base Fare Risk)
sample_options.notes: Emerging transpacific options. Base fare looks attractive but total cost rises after ancillary fees. Consider only if packing light and flexible.
